sections.heading: The national rate barely changed, but regions moved differently
sections.body: In 2025, 76.5 percent of live births and stillbirths had a postnatal check within the first 2 days after birth. That was close to the 75.5 percent level in 2022. But the steady national number hides much bigger changes by region. In 2025, regional levels ranged from 91.4 percent in Western Visayas to 54.1 percent in BARMM, a 37.3-point gap. This measure is less about a large national shift and more about where mothers and newborns are being reached consistently in the first days after birth.

sections.heading: Western Visayas and Davao had the highest 2025 levels
sections.body: Western Visayas had the highest level in 2025 at 91.4 percent. Davao Region followed at 89.0 percent. Northern Mindanao and Caraga were also high, at 86.5 percent and 86.2 percent. All of these regions were well above the national level of 76.5 percent. Because the highest levels are not limited to one island group, strong early postnatal contact appears in scattered parts of the country.

sections.heading: The lowest regions were far below the national level
sections.body: BARMM had the lowest level in 2025 at 54.1 percent. Cagayan Valley followed at 61.2 percent, and MIMAROPA was at 62.0 percent. Each of these regions was more than 14 points below the national level. The gap between Western Visayas, the highest region, and BARMM was especially wide at 37.3 points. This matters because the measure is about checks in the first 2 days, when missed visits can mean missed early support for mothers and newborns.

sections.heading: Some regions rose sharply while others fell
sections.body: Ilocos Region increased from 62.6 percent in 2022 to 81.1 percent in 2025. This was the largest gain among regions with comparable data. BARMM also improved, from 44.5 percent to 54.1 percent, but it still had the lowest level in 2025. Cagayan Valley moved in the opposite direction, falling from 79.9 percent to 61.2 percent. MIMAROPA also fell, from 75.8 percent to 62.0 percent. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ declined as well, from 79.0 percent to 72.3 percent, putting it below the national level in 2025.

sections.heading: The combined total changed very little from 2022
sections.body: For live births and stillbirths combined, the postnatal check level was 76.5 percent in 2025. The comparable level in 2022 was 75.5 percent, so the increase was 1.0 point. This is a small national change, especially next to the larger regional shifts. The total gives a stable national picture, while the subgroup tables show where differences remain.

sections.heading: Mothers under age 20 improved but still lagged
sections.body: Mothers under age 20 had a postnatal check level of 73.0 percent in 2025. That was lower than 77.0 percent among mothers age 20 to 34 and 76.7 percent among mothers age 35 to 49. The under-20 group improved from 67.5 percent in 2022, a 5.5-point gain. Even so, it remained the lowest of the three age groups in 2025. The differences by age are not large, but younger mothers still show the clearest disadvantage.

sections.heading: Urban and rural levels were nearly the same
sections.body: Urban mothers had a 2025 postnatal check level of 77.2 percent, compared with 75.9 percent among rural mothers. The gap was only 1.3 points, and both groups rose slightly from 2022. Rural areas increased by 1.3 points, while urban areas increased by 1.0 point. Living in an urban or rural area by itself does not explain the larger differences across regions.

sections.heading: Education differences were clearer than urban-rural differences
sections.body: Postnatal checks were highest among mothers with Grades 11 to 12 education, at 81.8 percent in 2025. The level was 79.0 percent among college-educated mothers and 77.8 percent among mothers with post-secondary education. Mothers with Grades 1 to 6 education were lower at 68.7 percent. The no-education row was 54.2 percent and has a caution marker in the source. Education shows a clearer pattern than the urban-rural split, but the smallest education category should be read carefully.

sections.heading: Wealth differences existed, but they were not a simple ladder
sections.body: The lowest wealth quintile had the lowest level in 2025 at 71.6 percent. The middle quintile was highest at 79.4 percent, followed closely by the second quintile at 79.0 percent and the fourth quintile at 78.5 percent. The highest quintile was 75.0 percent, which was below the middle groups instead of clearly above them. This means the wealth pattern was uneven, not a simple rise from poorest to richest.

takeaways: Nationally, postnatal checks in the first 2 days after birth were nearly unchanged, at 76.5 percent in 2025 for live births and stillbirths combined.
Regional gaps were much larger than the national change, ranging from 91.4 percent in Western Visayas to 54.1 percent in BARMM.
Ilocos Region improved sharply from 2022, while Cagayan Valley and MIMAROPA dropped well below their earlier levels.
Urban-rural differences were small, but younger mothers, mothers with less schooling, and the lowest wealth quintile still had lower levels than several comparison groups.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
